narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 36 and an undergraduate GPA of 3.00, you've worked hard to build a strong academic foundation, coming from a middle-income family and planning to attend law school full-time. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 54.8% chance of failure for you—high enough that it tipped the decision toward rejecting your application to protect the school's bar passage rates. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is well below the typical student's rank around the middle of their class and signaled to the model a concerning dip in performance later in your studies. This outweighed your stronger Year 1 decile of 1, which had a milder push toward failure compared to averages. On a positive note, your LSAT score, close to the dataset average of about 36.7, strongly nudged the prediction toward passing, showing your potential in standardized testing. Your undergraduate GPA, slightly under the typical 3.21, provided some helpful counterbalance but wasn't enough to offset the top concerns. Your middle-income background played a small role in pushing toward failure relative to higher-earning peers, while your full-time enrollment plans offered a minor boost since most successful students follow that path. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your later-year grades stand out as the key shortfall, as stronger performers across the board usually rank higher in school deciles. I know this rejection stings, especially with your solid test scores and dedication, but it's not a reflection of your overall worth or potential as a lawyer. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your upper-year deciles, perhaps seeking tutoring or study groups tailored to law school rigor, and reapply after demonstrating that upward trend—many students turn predictions like this around with targeted improvements. We're rooting for you to succeed on your next attempt.
